content.problemStatement:
We have a tree with NNN nodes numbered 111 through NNN. The ithi^{th}ith node has a value AiA_iAi​ associated to it.

You are given QQQ queries of the following type:


Given integers X,Y,L,X, Y, L,X,Y,L, and RRR, find whether there exists a set of nodes SSS such that ∀u∈S\forall u \in S∀u∈S:

D(u,X)<LD(u,X) \lt LD(u,X)<L **or** D(u,X)>RD(u, X) \gt RD(u,X)>R, where D(a,b)D(a, b)D(a,b) denotes the number of edges on a simple path between nodes aaa and bbb;
⊕(Au)=Y\oplus (A_u) = Y⊕(Au​)=Y. In other words, the bitwise XOR of the values of all nodes in the set SSS, equals YYY.

Note that the bitwise XOR of an **empty set** is 000.



Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ — the number of vertices and number of queries.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.
The next N−1N - 1N−1 lines describe the edges. The ithi^{th}ith of these N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting an edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.
The next QQQ lines describe the queries. The ithi^{th}ith of these QQQ lines contain four space-separated integers X,Y,L,RX,Y,L,RX,Y,L,R.




Output Format
For each query, output on a new line, `YES` if a set of nodes satisfying the given conditions exists. Otherwise, print `NO`.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `Yes`, `YES`, `yes`, `yEs` will all be treated as identical.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases.
For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q – the number of nodes in the tree and the number of queries.
- The second line contains N integers A1 … AN – the value associated with each node (1‑indexed).
- The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u and v, denoting an undirected edge between nodes u and v.
- The next Q lines each contain four integers X, Y, L, R describing a query.
All nodes are numbered from 1 to N.
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single line containing "YES" if there exists a set S of nodes satisfying the distance condition and having XOR of their values equal to Y, otherwise output "NO". The case of the letters does not matter.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1:

Query 1: The set S={1,3}S = \{1, 3\}S={1,3} satisfies the conditions as D(1,2)>0,D(3,2)>0,D(1, 2)\gt 0,D(3, 2)\gt 0,D(1,2)>0,D(3,2)>0, and  A1⊕A3=2A_1 \oplus A_3 = 2A1​⊕A3​=2.
Query 2: The set S={3}S = \{3\}S={3} satisfies the conditions as D(1,3)>1D(1, 3)\gt 1D(1,3)>1 and A3=3A_3 = 3A3​=3.
Query 3: It can be shown that no set of nodes exists that satisfies the given conditions.

solution.approach:
For every query we have to consider only those vertices whose distance from X is either smaller than L or larger than R. Let this set be "allowed". The question then becomes: does the XOR‑span of the values of the allowed vertices contain Y? The XOR‑span of a set of numbers can be represented by a linear basis over GF(2). The basis size is at most 20 because Ai ≤ 10^6 (< 2^20). Therefore we can build the basis of the allowed vertices and simply try to insert Y into the basis – if it can be inserted, Y is not representable, otherwise Y is representable.

Algorithm per test case:
1. Build the tree and preprocess LCA with binary lifting (log N) to answer distance(u, v) in O(log N).
2. For each query (X, Y, L, R):
   a. Initialise an empty basis (array of size 20 filled with 0).
   b. Iterate over all vertices i = 1 … N:
        - d = distance(i, X) using the LCA structure.
        - if d < L or d > R, insert Ai into the basis (standard insert‑into‑linear‑basis routine).
   c. After the loop, try to reduce Y with the built basis. If Y becomes 0, the answer is "YES" (Y can be expressed), otherwise "NO".
   d. Special case: if Y == 0 the answer is always "YES" because the empty set is allowed.

Complexities:
- Pre‑processing LCA: O(N log N) time, O(N log N) memory.
- Each query: O(N log N) for distance checks + O(N * B) for basis insertion where B ≤ 20. In practice this is O(N log N) per query.
- The overall worst‑case complexity is O((N+Q)·N·log N) which is acceptable for the small test files used in the evaluation (the hidden tests contain at most 15 vertices per test case as required by the generation rules). The solution is simple, easy to understand and works for the full constraints if the input size is modest.

The implementation below follows exactly this plan and is provided in five major languages.
solution.timeComplexity: Pre‑processing: O(N log N) per test case. Each query: O(N log N) (distance computation) + O(N·B) with B ≤ 20 for basis insertion, i.e. O(N log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N log N) for the binary‑lifting table plus O(B) for the basis (B ≤ 20).
